title: French yoghurt cake
subtitle: A simple one-bowl cake measured in yoghurt pots
excerpt: The classic French gateau au yaourt: a tender, faintly lemony sponge mixed in a single bowl using the empty yoghurt pot as the measure. Eight slices. In France it is often the first thing a child bakes. In any country it is one of the quickest cakes you can make.
sourceNotes: The French yoghurt cake (gateau au yaourt) is documented in early-twentieth-century French household cookery writing and is a staple of French domestic baking literature from the same period. Yoghurt as a baking ingredient appears in pre-1928 French and Eastern European cookery texts. Cross-referenced with early-twentieth-century French culinary periodicals available through the Bibliotheque nationale de France digitised collections.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze slices individually. Thaw at room temperature for 30 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps in a sealed container for 3 days. Slightly more moist on day 2.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven. Conventional 185°C. A skewer should come out with just a few dry crumbs.

glossaryTerms.definition: Plain yoghurt adds moisture and a slight tang to cakes. Its acidity reacts with bicarbonate of soda to release carbon dioxide, which contributes to the rise. The fat and protein in full-fat yoghurt help to tenderise the crumb. Yoghurt cakes are characterised by a close, even texture and a moist, slightly springy crumb that holds up well for several days.

content.text: The French yoghurt cake is a single-bowl recipe with a traditional twist: the empty yoghurt pot is used as the measure for the other ingredients. One pot of yoghurt, two pots of sugar, three pots of flour, half a pot of oil.
keeps the crumb tender and slightly springy. Eight slices, mixed in under 10 minutes.

Heat the oven to 175°C fan (185°C conventional). Grease and line the tin with baking paper. Whisk the yoghurt, eggs, oil, vanilla, and lemon zest together in a large bowl. Add the sugar and whisk until combined. Sift in the flour, baking powder, and salt and fold until no dry streaks remain. Do not overwork. Pour into the prepared tin.

Bake for 30 to 35 minutes, until the cake is golden, the top springs back when pressed, and a skewer inserted into the centre comes out with only a few dry crumbs. Cool in the tin for 10 minutes, then turn out onto a wire rack. Serve at room temperature, plain or with a dusting of icing sugar.
